annotations.label: Game of Life as cellular automaton

anchor.snippet: is a cellular automaton devised by the British mathematician

annotations.note: Cellular automata and the Game of Life are not defined anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Zero-player game

anchor.snippet: meaning that its evolution is determined by its initial state

annotations.note: No formalization of deterministic zero-player game evolution for cellular automata exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infinite grid, two cell states

anchor.snippet: is played on an infinite square grid, with each cell being in one of two states

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of a two-state cell configuration on an infinite square grid for cellular automata.

annotations.label: Moore neighborhood evolution

anchor.snippet: according to the number of live and dead cells in their Moore neighborhood

annotations.note: The Moore neighborhood and neighbor-count update rule are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pattern types

anchor.snippet: Patterns can be grouped into different types, such as still lifes

annotations.note: Mathlib contains no taxonomy of Game-of-Life patterns.

annotations.label: Life-like cellular automata

anchor.snippet: known as Life-like cellular automata

annotations.note: Life-like cellular automata are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Universe of the Game of Life

anchor.snippet: The universe of the Game of Life is an infinite, two-dimensional orthogonal grid of square cells

annotations.note: The two-dimensional infinite grid universe of Life is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Eight neighbours

anchor.snippet: Every cell interacts with its eight neighbours

annotations.note: The eight-neighbour adjacency of grid cells in Life is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transition rules

anchor.snippet: At each step in time, the following transitions occur

annotations.note: The B3/S23 birth/survival transition rules are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Seed and first generation

anchor.snippet: The initial pattern constitutes the seed of the system

annotations.note: No notion of a Life seed pattern or generation step exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: I-heptomino

anchor.snippet: the I-heptomino can be simulated

annotations.note: Specific Life patterns such as the I-heptomino are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Power of a universal Turing machine

anchor.snippet: the Game of Life has the power of a universal Turing machine

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes a universal partial recursive function and Turing machine models, but not the universality of the Game of Life itself.

annotations.label: Common pattern types

anchor.snippet: Common pattern types include still lifes

annotations.note: Still lifes, oscillators, and spaceships are not classified anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: R-pentomino stabilization

anchor.snippet: it takes 1103 generations to stabilize

annotations.note: The R-pentomino's 1103-generation stabilization is a Life-specific result absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Period of a pattern

anchor.snippet: Period refers to the number of ticks a pattern must iterate through before returning to its initial configuration

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of the period of a cellular-automaton pattern.

annotations.label: Pulsar period-3 oscillator

anchor.snippet: is the most common period-3 oscillator

annotations.note: The pulsar oscillator is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Methuselahs

anchor.snippet: Patterns which evolve for long periods before stabilizing are called Methuselahs

annotations.note: The Methuselah concept is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Diehard

anchor.snippet: Diehard is a pattern that disappears after 130 generations

annotations.note: The Diehard pattern is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Acorn

anchor.snippet: Acorn takes 5,206 generations to generate 633 cells

annotations.note: The Acorn methuselah is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conway's unlimited growth conjecture

anchor.snippet: Conway originally conjectured that no pattern can grow indefinitely

annotations.note: Conway's growth conjecture and its refutation are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gosper glider gun

anchor.snippet: produces its first glider on the 15th generation

annotations.note: The Gosper glider gun is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simkin glider gun

anchor.snippet: which releases a glider every 120th generation

annotations.note: The Simkin glider gun is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Block-laying switch engine

anchor.snippet: a configuration that leaves behind two-by-two still life blocks

annotations.note: The block-laying switch engine is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Minimal infinite-growth pattern

anchor.snippet: The first has only ten live cells, which has been proven to be minimal

annotations.note: Minimality of the ten-cell infinite-growth pattern is a Life-specific result absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Puffer trains and rakes

anchor.snippet: puffer trains , which move along leaving behind a trail of debris

annotations.note: Puffer trains and rakes are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Breeder with quadratic growth

anchor.snippet: the first pattern with an asymptotically optimal quadratic growth rate , called a breeder

annotations.note: Breeders and quadratic growth of Life patterns are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sliding block memory counter

anchor.snippet: This sliding block memory can be used to simulate a counter

annotations.note: Sliding-block memory constructions are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Turing completeness

anchor.snippet: it is Turing complete

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes that Turing machine models compute exactly the partial recursive functions, but not that the Game of Life is Turing complete.

annotations.label: Oblique spaceships

anchor.snippet: are commonly referred to as oblique spaceships

annotations.note: Oblique spaceships are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gemini spaceship

anchor.snippet: the first oblique spaceship, dubbed

annotations.note: The Gemini spaceship is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Knightship

anchor.snippet: a spaceship that moves two squares left for every one square it moves down

annotations.note: Knightships are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sir Robin elementary knightship

anchor.snippet: The first elementary knightship, Sir Robin

annotations.note: The Sir Robin knightship is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Universal constructor

anchor.snippet: A universal constructor can be built which contains a Turing complete computer

annotations.note: Von Neumann universal constructors in Life are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First self-replicator

anchor.snippet: built the first replicator in the Game of Life that creates a complete copy of itself

annotations.note: Self-replicating Life patterns are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 0E0P metacell

anchor.snippet: the 0E0P metacell, a metacell capable of self-replication

annotations.note: The 0E0P metacell is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Turing-completeness and arbitrary programs

anchor.snippet: the Game of Life is Turing-complete and may therefore execute arbitrary programs

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes a universal function executing arbitrary partial recursive codes, but not the Game of Life's Turing completeness.

annotations.label: Undecidability of termination

anchor.snippet: it is undecidable whether an arbitrary program executed in the Game of Life would ever terminate

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the abstract halting problem is undecidable, but not the corresponding statement about Game-of-Life programs.

annotations.label: Undecidability of target pattern

anchor.snippet: it is also undecidable whether the target pattern will ever appear

annotations.note: The general undecidability (halting problem) is formalized, but pattern-reachability undecidability in Life is not.

annotations.label: Emergence and self-organization

anchor.snippet: It provides an example of emergence and self-organization

annotations.note: Emergence and self-organization in cellular automata are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tendency toward symmetry

anchor.snippet: Small isolated subpatterns with no initial symmetry tend to become symmetrical

annotations.note: The empirical tendency of Life subpatterns toward symmetry is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Speed of light c

anchor.snippet: this velocity is said to be the cellular automaton speed of light and denoted c

annotations.note: The cellular-automaton speed of light is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Observer-viewpoint rule formulation

anchor.snippet: if the sum of all nine fields in a given neighbourhood is three, the inner field state for the next generation will be life

annotations.note: The neighbour-sum formulation of the Life rule is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rule-string notation B3/S23

anchor.snippet: The standard Game of Life is symbolized in rule-string notation as B3/S23

annotations.note: Rule-string notation for Life-like automata is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Life-like cellular automata (rule-string)

anchor.snippet: Cellular automata on a two-dimensional grid that can be described in this way are known as Life-like cellular automata

annotations.note: Life-like cellular automata defined by birth/survival rule-strings are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Highlife B36/S23

anchor.snippet: Highlife , is described by the rule B36/S23

annotations.note: The HighLife variant is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Count of Life-like rules

anchor.snippet: produce universes that are either too chaotic or too desolate to be of interest

annotations.note: The enumeration of Life-like rules is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Isotropic rulespace

anchor.snippet: A further generalization produces the isotropic rulespace

annotations.note: The isotropic non-totalistic rulespace is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elementary cellular automata

anchor.snippet: known as elementary cellular automata

annotations.note: One-dimensional elementary cellular automata are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multi-state generalization

anchor.snippet: instead of two states, live and dead , there are three or more

annotations.note: Multi-state cellular-automaton generalizations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sierpinski triangle from B1/S12

anchor.snippet: the automaton B1/S12 generates four very close approximations to the Sierpinski triangle

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Sierpiński topological space but not the Sierpinski triangle fractal nor its generation by a cellular automaton.

annotations.label: Immigration variation

anchor.snippet: Immigration is a variation that is very similar to the Game of Life

annotations.note: The Immigration variant is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: QuadLife variation

anchor.snippet: called QuadLife, involves four different on states

annotations.note: The QuadLife variant is not in Mathlib.
